entries.title: Ripley Scroll (WMS 693) — detail: the golden winged bird
entries.creator: George Ripley tradition (anonymous limner)
entries.summary:
A detail from the Wellcome Collection's *Ripley Scroll* (MS 693) showing the golden winged bird — the Bird of Hermes — one of the emblematic alchemical verse-scrolls associated with the English canon George Ripley.

## Iconography
A radiant winged bird, gilded and displayed against the scroll's vertical sequence of emblems and verses. In the Ripley scrolls the bird perches above or within the alchemical vessels and serpents that climb the long roll of vellum.

## Significance
The [[motif:bird of hermes|Bird of Hermes]] is the scroll's emblem of volatility — "the Byrd of Hermes is my name, eating my wings to make me tame," runs the famous verse. It figures the volatile spirit that must be repeatedly sublimed and "fixed" (its wings eaten) until the volatile and the fixed are reconciled in the stone. The Ripley scrolls survive in roughly two dozen copies, prized as the most spectacular objects of English alchemy.

## For artists & game designers
A single iconic creature — a self-devouring golden bird — that reads instantly as 'volatile spirit'; excellent as a summonable familiar or a transmutation-stage emblem.

citations.text: Anonymous, *Ripley Scroll* (Wellcome MS 693), George Ripley tradition.
Jennifer M. Rampling, *The Experimental Fire: Inventing English Alchemy, 1300–1700* (2020).
Wellcome Collection

entries.title: Ripley Scroll (WMS 693) — detail: the alchemist with the flask of the verses
entries.creator: George Ripley tradition (anonymous limner)
entries.summary:
A detail from the Wellcome *Ripley Scroll* (MS 693), one of the great emblematic alchemical rolls of the Ripley tradition, showing the bearded philosopher who presides over the scroll's topmost vision.

## Iconography
At the head of the roll a robed, bearded alchemist (often read as Hermes Trismegistus or Ripley himself) holds aloft a monumental flask. Within and below the great vessel unfold the scroll's nested scenes — figures on the rim, the philosophers' egg, the toad and serpents — while bands of English verse run alongside the images down the vellum.

## Significance
The presiding sage and his outsized flask establish the scroll's claim: the whole cosmos of the Work is contained in the philosophical vessel. The verses ascribed to George Ripley (c. 1415–1490), canon of Bridlington, guide the reader stage by stage through the *opus*. The image of the master displaying the vessel became the visual signature of the genre.

## For artists & game designers
A classic 'master and his great work' tableau — a sage lifting a world-containing flask — ideal as a mentor portrait or the framing art for an alchemy questline.

citations.text: Anonymous, *Ripley Scroll* (Wellcome MS 693), George Ripley tradition.
Jennifer M. Rampling, *The Experimental Fire: Inventing English Alchemy, 1300–1700* (2020).
Wellcome Collection

entries.title: The Ripley Scroll — the full emblematic roll
entries.creator: George Ripley tradition (anonymous limner)
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons / Wellcome.
entries.summary:
A view of a complete *Ripley Scroll* — the long vertical alchemical picture-poem of the George Ripley tradition, read from top to bottom as a single continuous emblem.

## Iconography
The roll stacks the genre's whole cast in sequence: at the top the presiding sage with his vast flask; descending from it the philosophical vessels and fountains, the [[motif:red and green lion|red and green lions]], the dragon, the toad swelling with poison, the black and white birds, and the Bird of Hermes, with banderoles of English verse threaded between every stage.

## Significance
The scroll's vertical format is itself meaningful: the Work is a graded ascent and descent, the matter circulating from grossest to most spiritual and back. Each emblem encodes a phase — putrefaction (the toad), the warring fixed and volatile principles (the lions and dragon), volatilisation and fixation (the birds). About two dozen Ripley scrolls survive; they are among the most ambitious visual statements of medieval and early-modern English alchemy.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made vertical 'skill tree' of alchemy: a single scroll whose stacked emblems map cleanly onto progression stages from rot to gold.

citations.text: Anonymous, *Ripley Scroll*, George Ripley tradition (15th–17th c.).
Jennifer M. Rampling, *The Experimental Fire: Inventing English Alchemy, 1300–1700* (2020).
Wellcome Collection

entries.title: Ripley Scroll (WMS 693) — detail: the fountain and the philosophical vessels
entries.creator: George Ripley tradition (anonymous limner)
entries.summary:
A detail from the Wellcome *Ripley Scroll* (MS 693) showing the fountain of mercurial waters and the philosophical vessels that descend from the great flask.

## Iconography
A tiered fountain pours its streams into basins, flanked by emblems of Sun and Moon and by the round glass vessels of the Work. Serpents or a dragon coil at the base of the structure; bands of verse explain the flow of "our water" through the stages.

## Significance
The fountain is the scroll's image of the *aqua philosophorum* — the philosophical mercury, the dissolving and conjoining water of the Work. Sun and Moon over the basins mark the union of the solar (sulphur, the fixed) and lunar (mercury, the volatile) principles in that water. The cascading design literalises *solve et coagula*: matter dissolved into the fountain and gathered again in the vessels below.

## For artists & game designers
A vertical fountain of glowing 'philosophical water' between sun and moon — a strong centrepiece for a mana-spring, scrying-pool, or alchemy-station backdrop.

citations.text: Anonymous, *Ripley Scroll* (Wellcome MS 693), George Ripley tradition.
Jennifer M. Rampling, *The Experimental Fire: Inventing English Alchemy, 1300–1700* (2020).
Wellcome Collection

entries.title: Ripley Scroll (Wellcome) — detail: the red and green lion
entries.creator: George Ripley tradition (anonymous limner)
entries.summary:
A detail from a Wellcome *Ripley Scroll* showing the lions of the Work — the green lion and the red lion that figure dissolution and the maturing tincture.

## Iconography
One or two heraldic lions, coloured green and red, set among the scroll's vessels and verses. The green lion is sometimes shown devouring the sun; the red lion stands for the perfected, reddened stage.

## Significance
The [[motif:green lion|green lion]] is among alchemy's most famous emblems: the crude, corrosive *menstruum* (often vitriol or an acid) that "devours the sun," dissolving gold into the philosophical water. Its reddening into the red lion marks the passage from dissolution toward the *rubedo*, the red stone. The colour-coded beasts let the unlettered reader track the Work's progress at a glance — a hallmark of the Ripley scroll's pedagogy.

## For artists & game designers
Two colour-keyed lions — green for corrosive dissolution, red for the finished tincture — a tidy pair of icons for opposed reagents or rival factions.

citations.text: Anonymous, *Ripley Scroll* (Wellcome), George Ripley tradition.
Jennifer M. Rampling, *The Experimental Fire: Inventing English Alchemy, 1300–1700* (2020).
Wellcome Collection

entries.title: Ripley Scroll (WMS 693) — detail: the toad and the putrefaction
entries.creator: George Ripley tradition (anonymous limner)
entries.summary:
A detail from the Wellcome *Ripley Scroll* (MS 693) showing the toad — the scroll's emblem of putrefaction and the black stage of the Work.

## Iconography
A bloated toad, sometimes shown swelling with poison or expiring on the ground, set within the scroll's vertical sequence near a band of verse. In the celebrated 'Tode' verse the creature drinks the juice of grapes until it bursts and rots.

## Significance
The toad is the *nigredo*: the matter killed, blackened and putrefied so that it may be reborn. "A toad full ruddy I saw, did drink the juice of grapes so fast..." — the verse describes the poisoning, death and final dissolution from which the white and red stones arise. The image makes vivid the alchemical axiom that there is no generation without prior corruption.

## For artists & game designers
A grotesque, poison-swollen toad as the 'rot/death' stage marker — a vivid before-image for a rebirth or purification mechanic.

citations.text: Anonymous, *Ripley Scroll* (Wellcome MS 693), George Ripley tradition.
Jennifer M. Rampling, *The Experimental Fire: Inventing English Alchemy, 1300–1700* (2020).
Wellcome Collection

entries.title: Ripley Scroll (Wellcome) — detail: the dragon at the base
entries.creator: George Ripley tradition (anonymous limner)
entries.summary:
A detail from a Wellcome *Ripley Scroll* showing the great dragon that anchors the foot of the roll — the scroll's emblem of the prime matter and the mercurial serpent.

## Iconography
A winged dragon or coiled serpent occupies the base of the scroll, sometimes biting its tail or set beneath the figures and verses that rise above it. It frequently breathes fire or stands upon a black ground.

## Significance
The dragon is the scroll's *materia prima* — the raw, fiery, mercurial substance from which the Work begins, the "venomous dragon" the verses warn must be killed and tamed. As an [[motif:ouroboros|ouroboros]]-like serpent it also figures the cyclical unity of the *opus*, the matter that devours and renews itself. Placed at the foot of the roll, it is the ground out of which the whole ascent of emblems grows.

## For artists & game designers
A foundational dragon coiled at the base of a tower of symbols — a natural 'root boss' or origin-monster from which a progression visibly springs.

citations.text: Anonymous, *Ripley Scroll* (Wellcome), George Ripley tradition.
Jennifer M. Rampling, *The Experimental Fire: Inventing English Alchemy, 1300–1700* (2020).
Wellcome Collection

entries.title: Ripley Scroll (Wellcome) — detail: the sun, moon and stars
entries.creator: George Ripley tradition (anonymous limner)
entries.summary:
A detail from a Wellcome *Ripley Scroll* showing the celestial register — Sun, Moon and stars — that governs the alchemical emblems below.

## Iconography
Personified or rayed faces of the Sun and Moon, with scattered stars, crown a portion of the roll above the vessels and beasts. The luminaries pour their influence down toward the philosophical work arrayed beneath them.

## Significance
The scroll insists that the laboratory mirrors the heavens. [[motif:sol and luna|Sol and Luna]] over the apparatus name the two great principles — solar sulphur and lunar mercury, gold and silver, fixed and volatile — whose marriage is the goal of the Work. The starry band asserts the Hermetic correspondence *as above, so below*, by which the operator's vessel draws down and concentrates celestial virtue.

## For artists & game designers
A compact celestial header — sun, moon and stars feeding power downward — useful as a banner motif above an alchemy or astrology interface.

citations.text: Anonymous, *Ripley Scroll* (Wellcome), George Ripley tradition.
Jennifer M. Rampling, *The Experimental Fire: Inventing English Alchemy, 1300–1700* (2020).
Wellcome Collection

entries.title: The Ripley Scroll (Huntington Library copy)
entries.creator: George Ripley tradition (anonymous limner)
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons / Huntington Library.
entries.summary:
The *Ripley Scroll* in the copy held by the Huntington Library — one of the roughly two dozen surviving witnesses to this spectacular English alchemical picture-poem.

## Iconography
The full vertical programme of the genre: the presiding sage and his great flask at the head; below, the fountain, the philosophical vessels, the red and green lions, the dragon, the toad and the Bird of Hermes, threaded throughout with English alchemical verse on banderoles.

## Significance
The survival of the scroll in multiple institutional copies (Wellcome, British Library, Huntington, Yale, Bodleian and others) lets scholars trace how the emblem-sequence was copied, varied and transmitted from the late fifteenth into the seventeenth century. Jennifer Rampling and others have used these copies to reconstruct the textual tradition behind the verses attributed to Canon George Ripley. The Huntington roll is a key American witness to that lineage.

## For artists & game designers
A second complete exemplar of the scroll — valuable when you want to compare variant renderings of the same emblem sequence for authenticity or for picking the cleanest details.

citations.text: Anonymous, *Ripley Scroll* (Huntington Library copy), George Ripley tradition.
Jennifer M. Rampling, *The Experimental Fire: Inventing English Alchemy, 1300–1700* (2020).
Wellcome Collection

entries.title: Ripley Scroll (WMS 693) — detail: the black and white birds
entries.creator: George Ripley tradition (anonymous limner)
entries.summary:
A detail from the Wellcome *Ripley Scroll* (MS 693) showing the paired birds — the black bird and the white bird — that mark the colour-changes of the Work.

## Iconography
Within or above one of the scroll's great vessels, birds rise and descend: a dark raven-like bird and a pale dove-like bird, sometimes shown flying up and down the neck of the flask. Verse-bands explain their alternating flight.

## Significance
The ascending and descending birds figure the repeated sublimation and condensation of the Work — the volatile spirit driven up and brought down again until purified. Their colours track the stages: the black bird the *nigredo* (the raven's head), the white the *albedo* (the dove, the white stone). The motion of the birds in the vessel is the scroll's way of showing *circulation*, the patient cycling that perfects the matter.

## For artists & game designers
Paired ascending/descending birds (black down, white up) inside a vessel — a clean animation cue for a purification or distillation loop.

citations.text: Anonymous, *Ripley Scroll* (Wellcome MS 693), George Ripley tradition.
Jennifer M. Rampling, *The Experimental Fire: Inventing English Alchemy, 1300–1700* (2020).
Wellcome Collection

entries.title: Ripley Scroll (WMS 693) — full roll with verses
entries.creator: George Ripley tradition (anonymous limner)
entries.summary:
A reproduction of the Wellcome *Ripley Scroll* (MS 693) as a whole — the long roll of emblems and English verse read continuously from the presiding sage at the top to the dragon at the foot.

## Iconography
The complete vertical sequence: the master and his great flask; the fountain and vessels; sun, moon and stars; the red and green lions; the toad; the black and white birds; the Bird of Hermes; and the dragon at the base — with verse banderoles running the length of the vellum.

## Significance
Seen whole, the scroll is a single synoptic emblem of the *opus alchymicum*: every creature and vessel is a stage, and the eye travels the Work from prime matter to perfected stone in one sweep. The verses, attributed to Canon George Ripley, supply the only sustained text — making the scroll a rare marriage of English vernacular alchemical poetry with monumental painted emblem. Wellcome MS 693 is among the best-preserved and most reproduced of the surviving rolls.

## For artists & game designers
The canonical 'whole scroll' reference: keep it on hand as the master layout when building any vertical, stage-by-stage alchemical interface.

citations.text: Anonymous, *Ripley Scroll* (Wellcome MS 693), George Ripley tradition.
Jennifer M. Rampling, *The Experimental Fire: Inventing English Alchemy, 1300–1700* (2020).
Wellcome Collection

entries.title: Ripley Scroll (Wellcome) — detail: the philosophers attending the vessel
entries.creator: George Ripley tradition (anonymous limner)
entries.summary:
A detail from a Wellcome *Ripley Scroll* showing small philosopher-figures grouped around (or standing on the rim of) the great philosophical vessel.

## Iconography
Diminutive robed figures attend the central flask — perched on its lip, gesturing toward its contents, or ranged about its base — within the scroll's column of emblems and verse. They personify the adepts and the principles enclosed in the vessel.

## Significance
The figures-on-the-vessel motif dramatises the idea that the Work is peopled: inside the glass the contending principles (often read as the male and female, Sol and Luna, sulphur and mercury) meet, struggle and conjoin. The watching philosophers also assert a chain of transmission — the wisdom passed from adept to adept, which the Ripley verses claim to preserve. It is a characteristically English elaboration of the Continental coniunctio imagery.

## For artists & game designers
Tiny attendant figures clustered on a giant flask — a charming scale-play motif for a 'homunculus' or 'soul in the bottle' moment.

citations.text: Anonymous, *Ripley Scroll* (Wellcome), George Ripley tradition.
Jennifer M. Rampling, *The Experimental Fire: Inventing English Alchemy, 1300–1700* (2020).
Wellcome Collection

entries.title: Ripley Scroll (Wellcome) — detail: the sphere of the elements
entries.creator: George Ripley tradition (anonymous limner)
entries.summary:
A detail from a Wellcome *Ripley Scroll* showing a banded sphere or philosophers' egg — the cosmos enclosed within the alchemical vessel.

## Iconography
A large orb divided into concentric bands or quarters, suggesting the elements and spheres, set among the scroll's vessels and verses. It reads at once as a globe of the world and as the sealed philosophical egg in which the matter is enclosed.

## Significance
The banded sphere expresses the doctrine of the [[motif:microcosm|microcosm]]: the sealed vessel contains, in little, the whole structure of the cosmos — the four elements and the heavens — so that perfecting the matter within is a re-making of creation. The *ovum philosophicum* (philosophers' egg) is the hermetically sealed flask in which the Work gestates; the scroll renders it as a small world about to hatch the stone.

## For artists & game designers
A banded cosmic egg/globe inside a vessel — a versatile icon for a 'world-seed', incubating artifact, or contained-universe item.

citations.text: Anonymous, *Ripley Scroll* (Wellcome), George Ripley tradition.
Jennifer M. Rampling, *The Experimental Fire: Inventing English Alchemy, 1300–1700* (2020).
Wellcome Collection

entries.title: Ripley Scroll (Wellcome) — detail: the verse banderoles
entries.creator: George Ripley tradition (anonymous limner)
entries.summary:
A detail from a Wellcome *Ripley Scroll* focused on its inscribed verses — the bands of English alchemical poetry that run beside the emblems.

## Iconography
Scrolling banderoles and text-panels carry lines of verse in a period English hand, threaded between the painted images down the roll. The lettering is part of the design, curling around vessels and figures.

## Significance
The verses are the scroll's voice: rhymed, riddling instructions attributed to Canon George Ripley that name 'our water', the toad, the bird and the lions, and exhort the reader to patience and secrecy. They make the Ripley scroll a hybrid object — emblem and poem at once — and are the principal evidence by which scholars such as Jennifer Rampling have reconstructed the work's textual history. The deliberate obscurity of the language enacts the alchemists' duty to conceal the Work from the unworthy.

## For artists & game designers
Authentic riddling 'flavour text' wound around imagery — a model for in-world inscriptions that reward close reading without giving the secret away.

citations.text: Anonymous, *Ripley Scroll* (Wellcome), George Ripley tradition.
Jennifer M. Rampling, *The Experimental Fire: Inventing English Alchemy, 1300–1700* (2020).
Wellcome Collection

entries.title: Alchemist holding a flask (Wellcome) — the presiding sage of the scroll
entries.creator: George Ripley tradition (anonymous limner)
entries.summary:
A Wellcome image of an alchemist holding a great flask — the presiding sage who stands at the head of the *Ripley Scroll* and displays the world-containing vessel.

## Iconography
A robed, bearded philosopher grasps an enormous round flask, within which the scenes of the Work are arrayed. He is the genius of the scroll, sometimes captioned as Hermes, sometimes as the alchemist-author, presenting the vessel to the viewer.

## Significance
The figure encapsulates the scroll's central claim: the entire *opus* — and indeed the cosmos — is enclosed within the philosophical glass that the master holds. As an image it became a generic emblem of the alchemist himself, widely reproduced apart from the full roll. The sage's calm display of the flask asserts mastery: the chaos of dragons, toads and lions below is governed from this single contemplative figure at the top.

## For artists & game designers
A portable, ready-cropped 'alchemist portrait' — a robed sage and his world-flask — ideal as an NPC avatar or shopkeeper icon lifted straight from the scroll.

citations.text: Anonymous, *Ripley Scroll* (Wellcome), George Ripley tradition.
Jennifer M. Rampling, *The Experimental Fire: Inventing English Alchemy, 1300–1700* (2020).
Wellcome Collection

entries.title: Alchemists revealing secrets from the Book of Seven Seals (Ripley Scroll)
entries.creator: George Ripley tradition (anonymous limner)
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons / Wellcome.
entries.summary:
A *Ripley Scroll* detail captioned as alchemists revealing the secrets of the "Book of Seven Seals" — an apocalyptic gloss on the scroll's topmost figures and verses.

## Iconography
Robed adepts gather about the scroll's great vessel and verse-panels, gesturing as though disclosing or unsealing hidden matter. The 'seven seals' caption draws on the imagery of Revelation, casting the alchemical secret as a sealed book now opened.

## Significance
The association with the Book of Seven Seals (Revelation 5) frames the Work as an apocalypse in the literal sense — an *unveiling*. Alchemical writers habitually borrowed scriptural and prophetic language to dignify and conceal their craft; the Ripley scroll's adepts 'opening the seals' present transmutation as a revealed mystery reserved for the elect. It typifies the easy traffic between alchemy and Christian eschatology in early-modern England.

## For artists & game designers
A scene of robed figures unsealing a hidden book of power — a strong 'lore reveal' or initiation tableau for an esoteric order.

citations.text: Anonymous, *Ripley Scroll*, George Ripley tradition; cf. Revelation 5 ("Book of Seven Seals").
Jennifer M. Rampling, *The Experimental Fire: Inventing English Alchemy, 1300–1700* (2020).
Wellcome Collection
